cards.front: Who is credited with creating one of the first periodic tables and arranging elements by increasing atomic mass?

What is the trend for atomic radius across a period (left to right)?
Why does atomic radius decrease across a period?
cards.back: Increasing nuclear charge pulls electrons closer

cards.front: What is the trend for atomic radius down a group?
Why does atomic radius increase down a group?
What is ionization energy?
cards.back: The energy required to remove one electron from a neutral atom

cards.front: What is the trend for ionization energy across a period (left to right)?
What is the trend for ionization energy down a group?
Why does ionization energy decrease down a group?
cards.back: Electrons are farther from the nucleus and easier to remove

cards.front: What is electronegativity?
cards.back: The ability of an atom to attract electrons in a chemical bond

cards.front: What is the trend for electronegativity across a period (left to right)?
What is the trend for electronegativity down a group?
Which element has the highest electronegativity?
What is electron affinity?
cards.back: The energy change when an atom gains an electron

cards.front: What is the trend for electron affinity across a period (left to right)?
What is the trend for electron affinity down a group?
What is metallic character?
cards.back: How readily an atom loses electrons to form positive ions

cards.front: What is the trend for metallic character across a period (left to right)?
What is the trend for metallic character down a group?
Where are metals located on the periodic table?
Where are nonmetals located on the periodic table?
Where are metalloids located on the periodic table?
cards.back: Along the staircase line between metals and nonmetals

cards.front: Name three elements that are metalloids.
What is the trend for ionic radius compared to atomic radius for cations?
cards.back: Cations are smaller than their neutral atoms

cards.front: What is the trend for ionic radius compared to atomic radius for anions?
cards.back: Anions are larger than their neutral atoms
